items.title: Etiological agent
items.contentHtml:
Sarcoptes scabiei var. hominis

  • It is an obligate mite of humans, with a complete life cycle (egg, larva, nymph, adult) in 10-17 days.
  • The fertilized female excavates tunnels in the stratum corneum to deposit 2-3 eggs/day.
  • Transmission requires skin-to-skin contact. prolonged(15-20 min); Fomites contribute little except in crusted scabies.
  • The infestation presents with delayed type intravenous hypersensitivity to antigens of the mite, its feces and eggs.
  • Certain diagnosis: visualization of the mite, eggs or scabbards in furrow scraping.

items.title: Cardinal symptom
items.contentHtml:
Intense nocturnal itching

  • It is the most characteristic and constant symptom, predominantly nocturnal due to greater mite activity and release of antigens.
  • It is due to the type intravenous hypersensitivity reaction (delayed) that appears 2-6 weeks after the primary infestation; in reinfestation, hours.
  • Itching persists up to 2 weeks after successful treatment due to antigenic remains, but if >2 weeks it suggests therapeutic failure or reinfestation.
  • Differential diagnosis: atopic dermatitis, insect prurigo, dermatitis herpetiformis, folliculitis.
  • In children, itching can be generalized and affect the scalp, palms and soles.

items.title: Pathognomonic lesion
items.contentHtml:
Acarine grooves or tunnels

  • They are serpiginous lines measuring 2-15 mm, grayish or white, raised, with a vesicle at the distal end where the female is located.
  • They are typically located in interdigital folds, wrists, elbows, armpits, waist, buttocks, periumbilical area, male genitals and female areolas.
  • In infants, they appear on the palms, soles, scalp and face.
  • Furrow scraping: diagnostic technique with scalpel and mineral oil, microscopic visualization at 10x of mites, eggs or scybales (sensitivity ~50-80%).
  • dermatoscopy shows the sign of delta (darker mite head and triangular body) with high sensitivity.

items.title: Classic topography
items.contentHtml:
Distribution in interdigital folds

  • It is the most frequent and suggestive location of scabies; the grooves appear between the fingers of the hands, especially the 3rd and 4th spaces.
  • The symmetrical distribution and in folds (wrists, armpits, groin, periumbilical region, submammary area) is key for clinical diagnosis.
  • In adults, it respects the face, scalp and upper back; in children and the elderly the distribution is more extensive.
  • Trap examination: the absence of lesions in interdigital folds does not rule out scabies, especially in crusted scabies or immunocompromised patients.
  • Itching in sexual partners or cohabitants with similar lesions reinforces the diagnosis.

items.foco: Most common cause of Norwegian scabies
items.title: Crusted scabies
items.contentHtml:
Most common cause of Norwegian scabies

  • Norwegian scabies (crusted) is associated with cellular immunosuppression (human immunodeficiency virus, leukemia, lymphoma, transplant), malnutrition, use of systemic or topical corticosteroids, and neurological or sensory disability (Down syndrome, dementia).
  • It is characterized by massive hyperkeratosis, scabs. thick and thousands of mites (up to 2 million), with high contagiousness.
  • The most common cause is human immunodeficiency virus infection with low CD4 count, especially <200 cells/μL.
  • Itching may be minimal or absent due to immune hyporeactivity.
  • Treatment: requires a combination of topical permethrin 5% (in the entire body surface, including face and scalp) and oral ivermectin 200 μg/kg in repeated doses (days 1, 2 and 14).

items.title: First line
items.contentHtml:
Treatment of choice: topical permethrin 5%

  • Topical permethrin 5% is the drug of choice due to its high efficacy (>90%), low toxicity and ovicidal and adulticidal action.
  • Apply to dry skin from the neck to the feet (including folds, nails and subungual region), leave it to act for 8-14 hours and then wash.
  • In children and elderly also apply to the face and scalp, avoiding eyes and mucous membranes.
  • Repeat the application after 7-14 days to eliminate mites hatched from residual eggs.
  • Alternatives: 5-10% sulfur cream in children under 2 months or pregnant; lindane (contraindicated in children and pregnant women due to neurotoxicity).

items.title: Second line
items.contentHtml:
Oral ivermectin in resistant cases

  • Oral ivermectin (200 μg/kg in a single dose) is indicated in cases of therapeutic failure, crusted scabies, institutional outbreaks or contraindication to topical treatment.
  • It is repeated after 7-14 days to cover the life cycle of the mite; in crusted scabies, additional doses are required (days 1, 2 and 14).
  • It is not approved in pregnancy, breastfeeding or children <15 kg (off-label use with caution).
  • Resistance to permethrin has been described in some regions; Oral ivermectin is the alternative of choice in these cases.
  • Adverse effects: headache, myalgia, exacerbation of pruritus due to massive death of mites (Mazotti-like reaction).

items.title: Neonatal differential diagnosis
items.contentHtml:
Cradle cradle in neonates

  • Cradle crusta (neonatal seborrheic dermatitis) presents as yellowish, greasy crusts on the scalp, without furrows or intense itching, and does not affect the palms or soles.
  • In neonates, scabies can simulate cradle crust, but is differentiated by the presence of furrows, erythematous papules, vesicles and intense itching (sometimes irritability).
  • Neonatal scabies affects scalp, face, palms and soles; The cradle cap respects the palms and soles.
  • Examination trap: Do not confuse neonatal scabies with atopic dermatitis or impetigo; sulcus scraping confirms the diagnosis.
  • Neonatal treatment: topical permethrin 5% on head and body (avoid eyes) or precipitated sulfur 5% in Vaseline.

items.title: Crusted scabies
items.contentHtml:
Hyperinfection syndrome in immunocompromised patients

  • Crusted scabies (Norwegian) is a hyperinfesting form with a massive parasite load, which in immunocompromised patients can be complicated by bacterial superinfection (Staphylococcus aureus, Streptococcus pyogenes).
  • Hematogenous dissemination is rare but possible; Cases of sepsis due to mites or secondary bacteria have been described.
  • The diagnosis is suspected in the presence of thick scabs, mild or absent pruritus, and high contagiousness (outbreaks in health personnel).
  • Management: contact isolation, combined treatment (topical permethrin 5% + oral ivermectin), and antibiotics if present superinfection.
  • Prevention: treat all contacts (cohabitants, healthcare personnel) and wash bedding and clothing at >60°C.

items.foco: Treatment of contacts and washing of clothes
items.title: Control measures
items.contentHtml:
Treatment of contacts and washing of clothes

  • All cohabitants and sexual contacts of the patient should receive simultaneous treatment, even if they are asymptomatic (incubation period of 2-6 weeks).
  • Treatment of contacts reduces reinfestation and breaks the chain of transmission; topical permethrin 5% is applied in a single prophylactic dose.
  • Bedding, towels and clothing used in the last 3 days should be washed in a hot cycle (>60°C) and dried at high temperature or stored in a sealed bag for 72 hours (the mite does not survive >48-72 hours outside the host).
  • Trap examination: no it is not necessary to fumigate the home or wash non-textile objects; Fomite transmission is minimal except in crusted scabies.
  • In institutional outbreaks (nursing homes, prisons), it is recommended to treat the entire floor or unit and repeat after 7-14 days.
